var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
from pathlib import Path

# Load books data (full query)
books_file = "/tmp/tmp5h1_3n8i.json"
if Path(books_file).exists():
    with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
else:
    # Fallback to variable
    books_data = var_functions.query_db:48

print(f"Books data loaded: {len(books_data)} records")
print(f"Sample book: {books_data[0] if books_data else 'No data'}")

# Load reviews data (limited query)
reviews_file = "/tmp/tmpm1s2wxc8.json"
if Path(reviews_file).exists():
    with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
        reviews_data = json.load(f)
else:
    reviews_data = var_functions.query_db:49

print(f"\nReviews data loaded: {len(reviews_data)} records")
print(f"Sample review: {reviews_data[0] if reviews_data else 'No data'}")

# Debug: Check year extraction
year_debug = []
for book in books_data[:50]:
    details = str(book.get('details', ''))
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    if years:
        year_int = min(int(y) for y in years if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023)
        year_debug.append({'id': book['book_id'], 'year': year_int, 'decade': (year_int//10)*10})

print(f"\nBooks with extractable years (first 50): {len(year_debug)}")
print(f"Sample: {year_debug[:5]}")

# Debug: Check review mapping
review_debug = []
for review in reviews_data[:20]:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id', '')
    numeric_part = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', '')
    book_id = f'bookid_{numeric_part}'
    review_debug.append({
        'purchase_id': purchase_id,
        'book_id': book_id,
        'rating': review.get('rating')
    })

print(f"\nReview to book mapping (first 20):")
for item in review_debug:
    print(item)

result = {
    'total_books': len(books_data),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews_data),
    'books_with_years_sample': len(year_debug),
    'review_mapping_sample': len(review_debug)
}
print("\n__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.execute_python:52.error: No decades with enough rated books found

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
from pathlib import Path

# Load books data (full query)
books_file = "/tmp/tmp5h1_3n8i.json"
if Path(books_file).exists():
    with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
else:
    # Fallback to variable
    books_data = var_functions.query_db:48

print(f"Books data loaded: {len(books_data)} records")
print(f"Sample book: {books_data[0] if books_data else 'No data'}")

# Load reviews data (limited query)
reviews_file = "/tmp/tmpm1s2wxc8.json"
if Path(reviews_file).exists():
    with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
        reviews_data = json.load(f)
else:
    reviews_data = var_functions.query_db:49

print(f"\nReviews data loaded: {len(reviews_data)} records")
print(f"Sample review: {reviews_data[0] if reviews_data else 'No data'}")

# Debug: Check year extraction
year_debug = []
for book in books_data[:50]:
    details = str(book.get('details', ''))
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    if years:
        year_int = min(int(y) for y in years if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023)
        year_debug.append({'id': book['book_id'], 'year': year_int, 'decade': (year_int//10)*10})

print(f"\nBooks with extractable years (first 50): {len(year_debug)}")
print(f"Sample: {year_debug[:5]}")

# Debug: Check review mapping
review_debug = []
for review in reviews_data[:20]:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id', '')
    numeric_part = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', '')
    book_id = f'bookid_{numeric_part}'
    review_debug.append({
        'purchase_id': purchase_id,
        'book_id': book_id,
        'rating': review.get('rating')
    })

print(f"\nReview to book mapping (first 20):")
for item in review_debug:
    print(item)

result = {
    'total_books': len(books_data),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews_data),
    'books_with_years_sample': len(year_debug),
    'review_mapping_sample': len(review_debug)
}
print("\n__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))

Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json, re
from collections import defaultdict

# Debug: Check available variables
print('Available variables:')
for k in sorted(locals().keys()):
    if k.startswith('var_'):
        val = locals()[k]
        print(f'  {k}: {type(val)}')
        if isinstance(val, list) and val:
            print(f'    Sample: {val[0]}')

print('\n--- Debug Analysis ---')

# Find books data
books_data = None
for k,v in locals().items():
    if isinstance(v, list) and v and isinstance(v[0], dict):
        if 'book_id' in v[0] and 'details' in v[0]:
            books_data = v
            print(f'Books found in {k}: {len(books_data)} records')
            break

# Find reviews data
reviews_data = None
for k,v in locals().items():
    if isinstance(v, list) and v and isinstance(v[0], dict):
        if 'purchase_id' in v[0] and 'rating' in v[0]:
            reviews_data = v
            print(f'Reviews found in {k}: {len(reviews_data)} records')
            break

# If not found in variables, try loading from files
if not books_data:
    import glob
    json_files = glob.glob('/tmp/*.json')
    print(f'JSON files in /tmp: {json_files}')

if books_data and reviews_data:
    print('\n--- Book Year Extraction ---')
    # Extract years from books
    books_with_years = 0
    decade_books = defaultdict(list)
    book_to_decade = {}
    
    for book in books_data:
        book_id = book.get('book_id')
        details = str(book.get('details', ''))
        years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
        if years:
            year = min(int(y) for y in years if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023)
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            decade_books[decade].append(book_id)
            book_to_decade[book_id] = decade
            books_with_years += 1

    print(f'Books with year data: {books_with_years}')
    print(f'Decades found: {len(decade_books)}')
    print('Top decades:')
    for decade, books in sorted(decade_books.items(), key=lambda x: -len(x[1]))[:10]:
        print(f'  {decade}: {len(books)} books')

    print('\n--- Review Mapping ---')
    # Map reviews to books
    book_ratings = defaultdict(list)
    for review in reviews_data:
        purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id', '')
        rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
        if purchase_id.startswith('purchaseid_'):
            book_id = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', 'bookid_', 1)
            book_ratings[book_id].append(rating)
    
    books_with_reviews = len(book_ratings)
    total_reviews = sum(len(r) for r in book_ratings.values())
    print(f'Books with reviews: {books_with_reviews}')
    print(f'Total review ratings: {total_reviews}')

    print('\n--- Overlap Analysis ---')
    # Find books with both year and reviews
    books_with_both = set(book_to_decade.keys()) & set(book_ratings.keys())
    print(f'Books with both year AND reviews: {len(books_with_both)}')
    
    if books_with_both:
        print('Sample:')
        for book_id in list(books_with_both)[:5]:
            decade = book_to_decade[book_id]
            rating_num = len(book_ratings[book_id])
            avg_rating = sum(book_ratings[book_id]) / rating_num
            print(f'  {book_id}: {decade}, reviews={rating_num}, avg={avg_rating:.2f}')

    print('\n--- Decade Statistics (rated books) ---')
    decade_rated_books = defaultdict(list)
    for book_id in books_with_both:
        decade = book_to_decade[book_id]
        decade_rated_books[decade].append(book_id)
    
    for decade, books in sorted(decade_rated_books.items(), key=lambda x: -len(x[1])):
        if len(books) >= 5:  # Show those with at least 5 books
            avg_ratings = [sum(book_ratings[b])/len(book_ratings[b]) for b in books]
            decade_avg = sum(avg_ratings) / len(avg_ratings)
            print(f'  {decade}: {len(books)} books, avg rating = {decade_avg:.3f}')

    result = {
        'books_with_years': books_with_years,
        'books_with_reviews': books_with_reviews,
        'books_with_both': len(books_with_both),
        'decades_with_books': len(decade_books),
        'decades_with_rated_books': len({d for d, books in decade_rated_books.items() if len(books) >= 10})
    }
else:
    result = {'error': 'Data not found'} 

print('\n__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json, re
from collections import defaultdict

# Debug: Check available variables
print('Available variables:')
for k in sorted(locals().keys()):
    if k.startswith('var_'):
        val = locals()[k]
        print(f'  {k}: {type(val)}')
        if isinstance(val, list) and val:
            print(f'    Sample: {val[0]}')

print('\n--- Debug Analysis ---')

# Find books data
books_data = None
for k,v in locals().items():
    if isinstance(v, list) and v and isinstance(v[0], dict):
        if 'book_id' in v[0] and 'details' in v[0]:
            books_data = v
            print(f'Books found in {k}: {len(books_data)} records')
            break

# Find reviews data
reviews_data = None
for k,v in locals().items():
    if isinstance(v, list) and v and isinstance(v[0], dict):
        if 'purchase_id' in v[0] and 'rating' in v[0]:
            reviews_data = v
            print(f'Reviews found in {k}: {len(reviews_data)} records')
            break

# If not found in variables, try loading from files
if not books_data:
    import glob
    json_files = glob.glob('/tmp/*.json')
    print(f'JSON files in /tmp: {json_files}')

if books_data and reviews_data:
    print('\n--- Book Year Extraction ---')
    # Extract years from books
    books_with_years = 0
    decade_books = defaultdict(list)
    book_to_decade = {}
    
    for book in books_data:
        book_id = book.get('book_id')
        details = str(book.get('details', ''))
        years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
        if years:
            year = min(int(y) for y in years if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023)
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            decade_books[decade].append(book_id)
            book_to_decade[book_id] = decade
            books_with_years += 1

    print(f'Books with year data: {books_with_years}')
    print(f'Decades found: {len(decade_books)}')
    print('Top decades:')
    for decade, books in sorted(decade_books.items(), key=lambda x: -len(x[1]))[:10]:
        print(f'  {decade}: {len(books)} books')

    print('\n--- Review Mapping ---')
    # Map reviews to books
    book_ratings = defaultdict(list)
    for review in reviews_data:
        purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id', '')
        rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
        if purchase_id.startswith('purchaseid_'):
            book_id = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', 'bookid_', 1)
            book_ratings[book_id].append(rating)
    
    books_with_reviews = len(book_ratings)
    total_reviews = sum(len(r) for r in book_ratings.values())
    print(f'Books with reviews: {books_with_reviews}')
    print(f'Total review ratings: {total_reviews}')

    print('\n--- Overlap Analysis ---')
    # Find books with both year and reviews
    books_with_both = set(book_to_decade.keys()) & set(book_ratings.keys())
    print(f'Books with both year AND reviews: {len(books_with_both)}')
    
    if books_with_both:
        print('Sample:')
        for book_id in list(books_with_both)[:5]:
            decade = book_to_decade[book_id]
            rating_num = len(book_ratings[book_id])
            avg_rating = sum(book_ratings[book_id]) / rating_num
            print(f'  {book_id}: {decade}, reviews={rating_num}, avg={avg_rating:.2f}')

    print('\n--- Decade Statistics (rated books) ---')
    decade_rated_books = defaultdict(list)
    for book_id in books_with_both:
        decade = book_to_decade[book_id]
        decade_rated_books[decade].append(book_id)
    
    for decade, books in sorted(decade_rated_books.items(), key=lambda x: -len(x[1])):
        if len(books) >= 5:  # Show those with at least 5 books
            avg_ratings = [sum(book_ratings[b])/len(book_ratings[b]) for b in books]
            decade_avg = sum(avg_ratings) / len(avg_ratings)
            print(f'  {decade}: {len(books)} books, avg rating = {decade_avg:.3f}')

    result = {
        'books_with_years': books_with_years,
        'books_with_reviews': books_with_reviews,
        'books_with_both': len(books_with_both),
        'decades_with_books': len(decade_books),
        'decades_with_rated_books': len({d for d, books in decade_rated_books.items() if len(books) >= 10})
    }
else:
    result = {'error': 'Data not found'} 

print('\n__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.query: SELECT purchase_id, CAST(rating AS FLOAT) as rating FROM review WHERE purchase_id IS NOT NULL;

exec_args.sql: SELECT purchase_id, CAST(rating AS FLOAT) as rating FROM review WHERE purchase_id IS NOT NULL;

result.preview: {"error": "No decades found with at least 10 distinct rated books"}

args.code:
import json, re
from collections import defaultdict
from pathlib import Path

# Load books data
books_file = "/tmp/tmpa5i9f0d4.json"
books_data = []
if Path(books_file).exists():
    with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
reviews_file = "/tmp/tmps4x5v3i5.json"
reviews_data = []
if Path(reviews_file).exists():
    with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
        reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Data validation
print(f'Books loaded: {len(books_data)}')
print(f'Reviews loaded: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Extract years and create mapping
book_to_decade = {}
decade_book_ids = defaultdict(list)
books_with_years = 0

for book in books_data:
    if not book or 'book_id' not in book:
        continue
    
    book_id = book['book_id']
    details = str(book.get('details', ''))
    
    # Find publication years (1900-2023)
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    
    if years:
        # Use the smallest year (usually the publication year)
        year = min(int(y) for y in years if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023)
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        decade_key = f"{decade}s"
        
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade_key
        decade_book_ids[decade_key].append(book_id)
        books_with_years += 1

print(f'Books with extractable years: {books_with_years}')
print(f'Decades with books: {len(decade_book_ids)}')

# Map reviews to books and aggregate ratings
book_ratings = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews_data:
    if not review or 'purchase_id' not in review:
        continue
    
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
    
    # Convert purchaseid_X to bookid_X
    if purchase_id.startswith('purchaseid_'):
        numeric_id = purchase_id.split('_')[1]
        book_id = f"bookid_{numeric_id}"
        book_ratings[book_id].append(rating)

print(f'Books with reviews: {len(book_ratings)}')

# Calculate average rating per book
book_avg_ratings = {}
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if ratings:
        book_avg_ratings[book_id] = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)

# Calculate decade averages (only for decades with >=10 books that have ratings)
decade_stats = {}
for decade, book_ids in decade_book_ids.items():
    # Only consider books that have ratings
    rated_books = [b for b in book_ids if b in book_avg_ratings]
    
    if len(rated_books) >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(book_avg_ratings[b] for b in rated_books) / len(rated_books)
        decade_stats[decade] = {
            'average_rating': avg_rating,
            'rated_books_count': len(rated_books),
            'total_books_in_decade': len(book_ids)
        }

print(f'Eligible decades (>=10 rated books): {len(decade_stats)}')

# Find the decade with highest average rating
if decade_stats:
    best_decade = max(decade_stats.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]['average_rating'])
    result = {
        'decade': best_decade[0],
        'average_rating': round(best_decade[1]['average_rating'], 3),
        'rated_books_count': best_decade[1]['rated_books_count'],
        'total_books_in_decade': best_decade[1]['total_books_in_decade']
    }
else:
    result = {'error': 'No decades found with at least 10 distinct rated books'}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.execute_python:52.error: No decades with enough rated books found

val_args.code:
import json, re
from collections import defaultdict
from pathlib import Path

# Load books data
books_file = "/tmp/tmpa5i9f0d4.json"
books_data = []
if Path(books_file).exists():
    with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
reviews_file = "/tmp/tmps4x5v3i5.json"
reviews_data = []
if Path(reviews_file).exists():
    with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
        reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Data validation
print(f'Books loaded: {len(books_data)}')
print(f'Reviews loaded: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Extract years and create mapping
book_to_decade = {}
decade_book_ids = defaultdict(list)
books_with_years = 0

for book in books_data:
    if not book or 'book_id' not in book:
        continue
    
    book_id = book['book_id']
    details = str(book.get('details', ''))
    
    # Find publication years (1900-2023)
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    
    if years:
        # Use the smallest year (usually the publication year)
        year = min(int(y) for y in years if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023)
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        decade_key = f"{decade}s"
        
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade_key
        decade_book_ids[decade_key].append(book_id)
        books_with_years += 1

print(f'Books with extractable years: {books_with_years}')
print(f'Decades with books: {len(decade_book_ids)}')

# Map reviews to books and aggregate ratings
book_ratings = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews_data:
    if not review or 'purchase_id' not in review:
        continue
    
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
    
    # Convert purchaseid_X to bookid_X
    if purchase_id.startswith('purchaseid_'):
        numeric_id = purchase_id.split('_')[1]
        book_id = f"bookid_{numeric_id}"
        book_ratings[book_id].append(rating)

print(f'Books with reviews: {len(book_ratings)}')

# Calculate average rating per book
book_avg_ratings = {}
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if ratings:
        book_avg_ratings[book_id] = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)

# Calculate decade averages (only for decades with >=10 books that have ratings)
decade_stats = {}
for decade, book_ids in decade_book_ids.items():
    # Only consider books that have ratings
    rated_books = [b for b in book_ids if b in book_avg_ratings]
    
    if len(rated_books) >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(book_avg_ratings[b] for b in rated_books) / len(rated_books)
        decade_stats[decade] = {
            'average_rating': avg_rating,
            'rated_books_count': len(rated_books),
            'total_books_in_decade': len(book_ids)
        }

print(f'Eligible decades (>=10 rated books): {len(decade_stats)}')

# Find the decade with highest average rating
if decade_stats:
    best_decade = max(decade_stats.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]['average_rating'])
    result = {
        'decade': best_decade[0],
        'average_rating': round(best_decade[1]['average_rating'], 3),
        'rated_books_count': best_decade[1]['rated_books_count'],
        'total_books_in_decade': best_decade[1]['total_books_in_decade']
    }
else:
    result = {'error': 'No decades found with at least 10 distinct rated books'}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

import json, re
from collections import defaultdict
from pathlib import Path

# Load the actual data files
books_file = "/tmp/tmp7z7j_1nz.json"
books_data = []
if Path(books_file).exists():
    with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)

reviews_file = "/tmp/tmpb_4i5h4h.json"
reviews_data = []
if Path(reviews_file).exists():
    with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
        reviews_data = json.load(f)

print(f'Total books: {len(books_data)}')
print(f'Total reviews: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Check book IDs
book_ids = [b.get('book_id') for b in books_data if b and 'book_id' in b]
print(f'Book IDs sample: {book_ids[:10]}')

# Check review purchase IDs
review_ids = [r.get('purchase_id') for r in reviews_data if r and 'purchase_id' in r]
print(f'Review purchase IDs sample: {review_ids[:10]}')

# Convert purchase IDs to book IDs and check overlap
converted_ids = []
for purchase_id in review_ids:
    if purchase_id and purchase_id.startswith('purchaseid_'):
        numeric_part = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', '')
        book_id = f'bookid_{numeric_part}'
        converted_ids.append(book_id)

print(f'Converted review IDs sample: {converted_ids[:10]}')

# Find overlap
overlap = set(book_ids) & set(converted_ids)
print(f'Books with reviews (overlap): {len(overlap)}')
print(f'Overlapping IDs sample: {list(overlap)[:10]}')

# Check year extraction for overlapping books
books_with_years = {}
for book in books_data:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    if book_id in overlap:
        details = str(book.get('details', ''))
        years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
        if years:
            year = min(int(y) for y in years if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023)
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            books_with_years[book_id] = decade

print(f'Overlapping books with year info: {len(books_with_years)}')

# Group by decade
decade_counts = defaultdict(int)
for book_id, decade in books_with_years.items():
    decade_counts[decade] += 1

print('Books by decade (overlapping):')
for decade, count in sorted(decade_counts.items()):
    print(f'  {decade}: {count}')

result = {
    'total_books': len(books_data),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews_data),
    'books_with_reviews': len(overlap),
    'books_with_years_and_reviews': len(books_with_years),
    'decades_covered': len(decade_counts),
    'decade_breakdown': dict(decade_counts)
}

print('\n__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json, re, glob, os
from collections import defaultdict

# Debug: Find all JSON files
print('=== JSON Files in /tmp ===')
json_files = glob.glob('/tmp/*.json')
for f in json_files:
    print(f'  {f} (size: {os.path.getsize(f)} bytes)')

# Try to load books data
books_data = []
reviews_data = []

for f in json_files:
    try:
        with open(f, 'r') as file:
            data = json.load(file)
            if data and isinstance(data, list):
                first_item = data[0]
                if isinstance(first_item, dict):
                    if 'book_id' in first_item and 'details' in first_item:
                        books_data = data
                        print(f'\n✓ Books loaded from: {f} ({len(books_data)} records)')
                        print(f'  Sample: {first_item[\"book_id\"]} - {first_item[\"details\"][:60]}...')
                    elif 'purchase_id' in first_item and 'rating' in first_item:
                        reviews_data = data
                        print(f'\n✓ Reviews loaded from: {f} ({len(reviews_data)} records)')
                        print(f'  Sample: {first_item[\"purchase_id\"]} - rating: {first_item[\"rating\"]}')
    except Exception as e:
        continue

print(f'\n=== Final Counts ===')
print(f'Books: {len(books_data)}')
print(f'Reviews: {len(reviews_data)}')

result = {
    'books_loaded': len(books_data) > 0,
    'reviews_loaded': len(reviews_data) > 0,
    'books_count': len(books_data),
    'reviews_count': len(reviews_data)
}
print('\\n__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

import json, re, glob, os
from collections import defaultdict

# Debug: Find all JSON files
print('=== JSON Files in /tmp ===')
json_files = glob.glob('/tmp/*.json')
for f in json_files:
    print(f'  {f} (size: {os.path.getsize(f)} bytes)')

# Try to load books data
books_data = []
reviews_data = []

for f in json_files:
    try:
        with open(f, 'r') as file:
            data = json.load(file)
            if data and isinstance(data, list):
                first_item = data[0]
                if isinstance(first_item, dict):
                    if 'book_id' in first_item and 'details' in first_item:
                        books_data = data
                        print(f'\n✓ Books loaded from: {f} ({len(books_data)} records)')
                        print(f'  Sample: {first_item[\"book_id\"]} - {first_item[\"details\"][:60]}...')
                    elif 'purchase_id' in first_item and 'rating' in first_item:
                        reviews_data = data
                        print(f'\n✓ Reviews loaded from: {f} ({len(reviews_data)} records)')
                        print(f'  Sample: {first_item[\"purchase_id\"]} - rating: {first_item[\"rating\"]}')
    except Exception as e:
        continue

print(f'\n=== Final Counts ===')
print(f'Books: {len(books_data)}')
print(f'Reviews: {len(reviews_data)}')

result = {
    'books_loaded': len(books_data) > 0,
    'reviews_loaded': len(reviews_data) > 0,
    'books_count': len(books_data),
    'reviews_count': len(reviews_data)
}
print('\\n__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
import pathlib

# Load books data
books_json = '/tmp/tmp7z7j_1nz.json'
books = []
if pathlib.Path(books_json).exists():
    with open(books_json, 'r') as f:
        books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data  
reviews_json = '/tmp/tmpb_4i5h4h.json'
reviews = []
if pathlib.Path(reviews_json).exists():
    with open(reviews_json, 'r') as f:
        reviews = json.load(f)

print(f'Loaded {len(books)} books and {len(reviews)} reviews')

# Extract publication year and decade for each book
book_decade = {}
decade_books = defaultdict(list)

for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    if years:
        year = min(int(y) for y in years if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023)
        decade = f"{(year // 10) * 10}s"
        book_decade[book['book_id']] = decade
        decade_books[decade].append(book['book_id'])

# Map purchase_id to book_id and collect ratings
book_ratings = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    book_id = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', 'bookid_', 1)
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    book_ratings[book_id].append(rating)

# Calculate average rating per book
book_avg = {bid: sum(r)/len(r) for bid, r in book_ratings.items()}

# Calculate decade averages (only decades with >=10 rated books)
decade_avg = {}
for decade, book_ids in decade_books.items():
    rated_books = [bid for bid in book_ids if bid in book_avg]
    if len(rated_books) >= 10:
        decade_avg[decade] = sum(book_avg[bid] for bid in rated_books) / len(rated_books)

# Find best decade
if decade_avg:
    best = max(decade_avg.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
    result = {
        'decade': best[0],

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.execute_python:68.error: No decades found with at least 10 distinct rated books

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
import pathlib

# Load books data
books_json = '/tmp/tmp7z7j_1nz.json'
books = []
if pathlib.Path(books_json).exists():
    with open(books_json, 'r') as f:
        books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data  
reviews_json = '/tmp/tmpb_4i5h4h.json'
reviews = []
if pathlib.Path(reviews_json).exists():
    with open(reviews_json, 'r') as f:
        reviews = json.load(f)

print(f'Loaded {len(books)} books and {len(reviews)} reviews')

# Extract publication year and decade for each book
book_decade = {}
decade_books = defaultdict(list)

for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    if years:
        year = min(int(y) for y in years if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023)
        decade = f"{(year // 10) * 10}s"
        book_decade[book['book_id']] = decade
        decade_books[decade].append(book['book_id'])

# Map purchase_id to book_id and collect ratings
book_ratings = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    book_id = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', 'bookid_', 1)
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    book_ratings[book_id].append(rating)

# Calculate average rating per book
book_avg = {bid: sum(r)/len(r) for bid, r in book_ratings.items()}

# Calculate decade averages (only decades with >=10 rated books)
decade_avg = {}
for decade, book_ids in decade_books.items():
    rated_books = [bid for bid in book_ids if bid in book_avg]
    if len(rated_books) >= 10:
        decade_avg[decade] = sum(book_avg[bid] for bid in rated_books) / len(rated_books)

# Find best decade
if decade_avg:
    best = max(decade_avg.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
    result = {
        'decade': best[0],
        'average_rating': round(best[1], 3)
    }
else:
    result = {'error': 'No decade found'}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

args.code:
import json, re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
books = json.loads(open('/tmp/tmp7z7j_1nz.json').read())
reviews = json.loads(open('/tmp/tmpb_4i5h4h.json').read())

# More detailed debugging
print(f'Total books: {len(books)}')
print(f'Total reviews: {len(reviews)}')

# Extract years for all books
book_years = {}
decade_counts = defaultdict(int)
decade_book_ids = defaultdict(list)

for book in books:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    details = book['details']
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    if years:
        year = min(int(y) for y in years if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023)
        decade = f"{(year // 10) * 10}s"
        book_years[book_id] = {'year': year, 'decade': decade}
        decade_counts[decade] += 1
        decade_book_ids[decade].append(book_id)

print(f'\nBooks with year data: {len(book_years)}')
print('\nTop decades by book count:')
for decade, count in sorted(decade_counts.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])[:10]:
    print(f'{decade}: {count} books')

# Map reviews
book_reviews = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    book_id = 'bookid_' + purchase_id.split('_')[1]
    book_reviews[book_id].append(float(review['rating']))

print(f'\nBooks with reviews: {len(book_reviews)}')

# Find intersection and analyze decade distribution
overlap = set(book_years.keys()) & set(book_reviews.keys())
print(f'Books with both year and reviews: {len(overlap)}')

# Check which decades have enough intersecting books
decade_overlap = defaultdict(list)
for book_id in overlap:
    decade = book_years[book_id]['decade']
    decade_overlap[decade].append(book_id)

print('\nDecades with overlapping books:')
for decade, books in sorted(decade_overlap.items(), key=lambda x: -len(x[1])):
    if len(books) >= 5:  # Show decades with at least 5 books
        print(f'{decade}: {len(books)} books')

# Calculate averages for decades with >=10 books
decade_averages = {}
for decade, book_ids in decade_overlap.items():
    if len(book_ids) >= 10:
        ratings = []
        for book_id in book_ids:
            ratings.extend(book_reviews[book_id])
        if ratings:
            decade_averages[decade] = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)

print(f'\nDecades eligible for analysis (>=10 books): {len(decade_averages)}')
if decade_averages:
    best = max(decade_averages.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
    print(f'Best decade: {best}')

result = {
    'books_with_years': len(book_years),
    'books_with_reviews': len(book_reviews),
    'books_with_both': len(overlap),
    'eligible_decades': len(decade_averages),
    'decade_overlap': {d: len(b) for d, b in decade_overlap.items()}
}
print('\n__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
